subNodes.summary: B.5.2.0:9 - Consequences The benefit is a cleaner, less brittle entry contract for abduction. The trade-off is one additional explicit prompt supertype and one more declared publication form.

subNodes.summary: B.5.2.0:10 - Rationale This keeps lawful cue preservation and route publication able to dock into B.5.2 through a typed prompt form without anomaly inflation and without making A.16.0 mandatory.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.1 - F0 - Unstructured prose
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.1 - F0 - Unstructured prose Free natural language with unstable vocabulary, implicit assumptions, and no stable internal structure.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.2 - F1 - Scoped notes
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.2 - F1 - Scoped notes Still informal, but with stable topic focus and more consistent terminology. Scope is named even though criteria are not yet operationalized.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.3 - F2 - Structured outline
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.3 - F2 - Structured outline A recognizable template or full section shape exists. The artifact is coherent end-to-end, but acceptance criteria are still largely placeholders or informal.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.4 - F3 - Controlled narrative
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.4 - F3 - Controlled narrative Claims are expressed in constrained prose with stable interpretation. Acceptance or refusal conditions are visible in language, even if not yet fully predicate-like.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.5 - F4 - First-order constraints
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.5 - F4 - First-order constraints Critical claims can be rendered as explicit predicates or invariants over typed entities. Consistency and conflict are at least checkable in principle.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.6 - F5 - Executable math / algorithmics
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.6 - F5 - Executable math / algorithmics The artifact has declared executable semantics. Running the model, algorithm, or simulation is part of its meaning.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.7 - F6 - Hybrid formalism
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.7 - F6 - Hybrid formalism Several formal layers are coordinated explicitly, typically discrete plus continuous or several tightly coupled formal subsystems, with declared obligations between them.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.8 - F7 - Higher-order verified
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.8 - F7 - Higher-order verified Core claims are encoded in a proof-capable higher-order setting and machine-checked against that logic kernel.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.9 - F8 - Dependent / constructive proofs
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.9 - F8 - Dependent / constructive proofs Programs-as-proofs or dependent-type artifacts carry the relevant property in their types or proof terms.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.10 - F9 - Univalent / higher foundations
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.10 - F9 - Univalent / higher foundations Higher-equality foundations are load-bearing. The artifact relies on a frontier-grade setting where equivalence is handled as structure-level identity.

subNodes.title: C.2.3:13.11 - Cross-anchor cautions
subNodes.summary: C.2.3:13.11 - Cross-anchor cautions - Execution is not proof. - Surface structure is not yet semantics. - Publishing or approval is not an anchor. - A local sub-anchor does not erase its parent anchor's meaning.

subNodes.summary: C.2.3:15.2 - Relation to G F concerns expression form; G concerns applicability or claim scope. Tightening scope may accompany a raise in F, but it is a separate change and must remain visible as such.

subNodes.summary: C.2.3:15.3 - Relation to R Higher F often makes evidence easier to formulate, test, or prove, but it does not create warrant strength by itself. Empirical freshness, corroboration, and bridge penalties remain R concerns.

subNodes.title: C.2.4:9 - Consequences
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:9 - Consequences The benefit is lawful publication of early cues and clearer threshold setting for repair. The trade-off is that authors must distinguish "not yet explicit" from "already formal".

subNodes.title: C.2.4:10 - Rationale
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:10 - Rationale AE is one basis slot in the declared language-state chart over U.CharacteristicSpace. Without it, A.16.0, A.16.1, and B.4.1 cannot state crisp entry, seam, and exit conditions.

subNodes.title: C.2.4:14.1 - Author prompt
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:14.1 - Author prompt To assign AE, ask: - is the trigger span stable? - are candidate anchors visible? - is there already a minimally relation-like skeleton? - is a normal form actually publishable, or only hinted?

subNodes.title: C.2.4:14.2 - Review prompt
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:14.2 - Review prompt A reviewer should reject AE claims that rely only on rhetorical confidence. The claimed level should be supported by anchors, slots, contrasts, exemplars, or explicit normal-form structure.

subNodes.title: C.2.4:14.3 - Threshold publication reminder
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:14.3 - Threshold publication reminder If AE determines whether an episteme stays in A.16.1, passes through B.4.1, or enters A.6.P, that threshold should be published explicitly and locally.

subNodes.summary: C.2.4:15.3 - Boundary reminder AE does not own closure, confidence, or warrant. If authors want those meanings, they must publish them through their own owners.

subNodes.title: C.2.4:16.2 - Threshold package for route change
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:16.2 - Threshold package for route change If entry from A.16.1 or B.4.1 into A.6.P depends on AE, publish the threshold together with the minimum articulation package required at crossing time.

subNodes.title: C.2.4:16.3 - Evidence-limited rise rule
subNodes.summary: C.2.4:16.3 - Evidence-limited rise rule AE may rise only as far as the published anchors, slots, and contrasts warrant. Stylistic polish, templates, or rhetorical confidence do not raise AE on their own.

subNodes.title: C.2.5:9 - Consequences
subNodes.summary: C.2.5:9 - Consequences The benefit is lawful handling of stabilization, commitment, and reopening. The trade-off is more explicit state declaration and more explicit retreat records.

subNodes.title: C.2.5:10 - Rationale
subNodes.summary: C.2.5:10 - Rationale Closure is the route-governance basis slot that complements articulation within the declared language-state chart over U.CharacteristicSpace. A.16.0 and its seam species need both.

subNodes.title: C.2.5:11 - SoTA-Echoing
subNodes.summary: C.2.5:11 - SoTA-Echoing The facet aligns with iterative design, open-world reasoning, and exploratory search practices where closure is a governance choice rather than a hidden by-product.

subNodes.summary: C.2.5:13.3 - Reopen case A route may move from CD4 back to CD2 when counter-evidence appears. A conforming publication does not hide this as embarrassment; it records the retreat as a lawful A.16.2 move.

subNodes.summary: C.2.5:14.2 - Review prompt A reviewer should ask whether closure is being inferred from tone, from hierarchy, or from social pressure rather than from an explicit narrowing of route or frame space.

subNodes.title: C.2.5:14.3 - Governance note
subNodes.summary: C.2.5:14.3 - Governance note Whenever CD materially affects gates, commitments, or late endpoint authority, the supporting guard or owner should be visible.

subNodes.summary: C.2.5:15.3 - Boundary reminder CD is not warrant strength and not a gate decision. It speaks only about the local fixity of the current episteme/publication path and its candidate space.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.1 - Same‑sense & same‑place
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.1 - Same‑sense & same‑place ContextOf(ℓ₁)=ContextOf(ℓ₂) ∧ senseOf(ℓ₁)=senseOf(ℓ₂) ⊢ mayRename(ℓ₁→ℓ₂) Reading: If two labels denote the same SenseCell in the same Context , a rename is legitimate.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.2 -Local alias
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.2 -Local alias ContextOf(ℓ₁)=ContextOf(ℓ₂) ∧ senseOf(ℓ₁)=senseOf(ℓ₂) ⊢ aliases(ℓ₁↔ℓ₂) Reading: Legacy synonym can be kept as a read‑path ; writing uses pref.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.3 - Split detection
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.3 - Split detection coversMultipleLocalSenses(ℓ) ⊢ splits(ℓ ⇒ {ℓA,ℓB,… }) Reading: If one label straddles several local senses, declare a split and prefer the new precise labels.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.4 - Merge admission
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.4 - Merge admission ContextOf(ℓA)=ContextOf(ℓB) ∧ senseOf(ℓA)=senseOf(ℓB) ⊢ merges({ℓA,ℓB} ⇒ ℓN) Reading: Once F.3 shows identity of sense within a Context, merging labels into one preferred label is safe.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.5 - Retirement
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.5 - Retirement misleading(ℓ) ∧ ¬∃ℓ' sameSense(ℓ,ℓ') ⊢ retires(ℓ) Reading: If a label misleads and has no single successor, retire it and point readers to relevant Contexts/rows.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.6 - Cross‑context guard
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.6 - Cross‑context guard ContextOf(ℓ₁) ≠ ContextOf(ℓ₂) ⊢ ¬mayRename(ℓ₁→ℓ₂) Reading: Different Contexts forbid rename/alias; any relation goes to Bridge (F.9).

subNodes.title: F.13:12.7 - Writing discipline
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.7 - Writing discipline thing t ⊢ writeWithPreferred(t) = pref(t) Reading: Normative prose uses the current preferred label; aliases are for reading.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.8 - Reading resolution
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.8 - Reading resolution legacyLabel ℓ ⊢ readResolve(ℓ) = ⟨thing, pref(thing), epoch?⟩ Reading: A reader can mentally resolve a legacy label to the thing and its present name, with epoch hint if needed.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.9 - Alias budget
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.9 - Alias budget aliasesFor(thing, register=r) = A ⊢ A ≤ 1 Reading: Keep at most one legacy alias per register (Tech/Plain) for any one thing.

subNodes.title: F.13:12.10 - Row‑level continuity
subNodes.summary: F.13:12.10 - Row‑level continuity rowOf(ℓA)=rowOf(ℓB)=R ∧ intension(R) stable ⊢ mayRenameRow(R,ℓB) Reading: A row label can change if the row’s membership/intension did not change; otherwise refactor rows first (F.7).
